agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental compliance, legal, and business policy change with severe downstream consequences.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Records / Data Retention / Compliance",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery: consignment details, proof of delivery, timestamps, parties involved, goods description, and associated financial data." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The business and legal rules governing how long records must be preserved before they may be purged." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that goods were delivered to the correct recipient — a legally significant document in dispute resolution and compliance." },
      { "term": "Limitation Period", "definition": "The statutory window during which a legal claim can be brought. In England/Wales this is typically 6 years for contract claims under the Limitation Act 1980." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations." },
      { "term": "Customs Declaration", "definition": "Import/export documentation that must be retained per EU and UK customs regulations, often linked to delivery records for cross-border shipments." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records that serve as evidence for tax, VAT, or customs purposes must be retained for the minimum period required by the applicable tax authority (HMRC: 6 years; EU member states: typically 5-10 years depending on jurisdiction).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Statutory obligation. Violation exposes the company to regulatory penalties, failed audits, and criminal liability. No VP can override tax law.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record linked to a VAT, tax, or customs transaction may be deleted before the jurisdiction-specific statutory retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery records must be retained for the full duration of the applicable civil limitation period to defend against contractual and tortious claims.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Destroying evidence within the limitation period exposes the company to indefensible legal claims and potential adverse inferences in litigation. UK Limitation Act 1980 sets 6 years for contract; some EU jurisdictions allow up to 10 years.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record may be deleted if it falls within the civil limitation period of the jurisdiction where the delivery occurred."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "B2B customers who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting must have access to that data for at least the duration of their own regulatory retention obligations.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual and commercial obligation, not a physical constraint. However, unilateral deletion would likely breach service agreements and destroy customer trust.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records accessible to B2B customers must not be purged before the longest retention period specified in any active B2B service agreement."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "GDPR does not mandate deletion after 90 days. It requires retention to be proportionate to purpose. Where a legitimate legal or compliance purpose exists, retention for years is lawful and required.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR Article 17(3)(b) and (e) explicitly exempt data necessary for legal claims or compliance obligations from the right to erasure. Premature deletion violates legal hold obligations.",
      "testable_assertion": "Data retention policy must document a lawful basis for the chosen retention period per GDPR Article 5(1)(e), and must not delete data that falls under a legal exemption to erasure."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Cross-border delivery records involving customs declarations must be retained per EU Union Customs Code (Article 51: minimum 3 years, often extended by member states to 10 years) and UK equivalents.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Customs authorities can audit retrospectively. Missing records result in penalties, duty reassessments, and loss of trusted trader status.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record associated with a customs declaration may be deleted before the customs retention period of the relevant jurisdiction has elapsed."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer is audited by their national tax authority 4 years after a delivery and requests the original delivery records as evidence.", "expected_behavior": "Records must be available. Under the 90-day policy, they would have been destroyed 3+ years earlier, making the customer's audit indefensible." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer files a breach-of-contract claim 5 years after a failed delivery.", "expected_behavior": "The claim is within the 6-year UK limitation period. The company needs the delivery record to mount a defense. Under the 90-day policy, the evidence is gone." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC conducts a VAT audit covering the past 4 years.", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records with VAT implications must be producible. A 90-day retention policy would mean 3+ years of records are missing, triggering penalties and estimated assessments." },
    { "scenario": "A customs authority audits cross-border shipments from 2 years ago.", "expected_behavior": "Full customs-linked delivery records must be available. Failure to produce them risks loss of Authorised Economic Operator (AEO) status and financial penalties." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery dispute is raised on day 91.", "expected_behavior": "Under the proposed policy, the record is already deleted. The company cannot resolve the dispute or defend itself, resulting in automatic liability." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Potential exposure includes: (1) Tax penalties from HMRC and EU authorities for missing records during audits — these can be percentage-of-revenue penalties. (2) Inability to defend contract claims within the 6-year limitation period, leading to default judgments. (3) Customs duty reassessments and loss of AEO/trusted trader status. (4) B2B customer churn as their own compliance is jeopardized. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine or lost enterprise contract.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL. (1) B2B customers will lose access to data they contractually depend on, generating support escalations, SLA breaches, and contract termination claims. (2) Internal finance, legal, and compliance teams lose the ability to perform retrospective audits. (3) Dispute resolution becomes impossible for any delivery older than 90 days.",
    "user": "HIGH. (1) B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries for returns, warranty claims, or disputes beyond 90 days. (2) B2B customers face compliance failures in their own operations, destroying trust in the platform. (3) Both segments lose confidence in the platform's reliability as a system of record.",
    "risk": "EXTREME. This change creates legal and regulatory exposure across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously. The framing as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' is itself a risk indicator — it suggests the compliance implications have not been evaluated. The company would be in active violation of tax retention laws in every EU/UK jurisdiction it operates in.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Legal & Compliance Team", "impact": "Loses ability to respond to legal claims, regulatory audits, and data subject access requests that reference historical deliveries. Directly liable for compliance failures." },
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Their own regulatory compliance is destroyed. They depend on delivery records for tax reporting, customs compliance, and audit trails. Likely breach of service agreements." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Lose proof-of-delivery records needed for disputes, warranty claims, insurance claims, and chargebacks beyond 90 days." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax Team", "impact": "Cannot produce records for VAT audits, tax filings, or financial reconciliation beyond 90 days. Direct exposure to HMRC and EU tax authority penalties." },
      { "group": "Engineering Team", "impact": "Will bear the operational burden of emergency recovery attempts, customer escalations, and eventual re-architecture when the policy is reversed." },
      { "group": "Executive Leadership", "impact": "Personal liability risk under UK and EU regulations for knowingly implementing a non-compliant data retention policy." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 40,
    "reasoning": "Confidence is LOW because the proposal contains a fundamental contradiction: it claims 'no business logic changes' while proposing to delete data that is legally required to exist. This contradiction means the spec is not implementable as described. Additionally, no legal review has been mentioned, and specific jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction retention requirements would need legal counsel to enumerate precisely. The 90-day figure appears to have no legal basis whatsoever.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC requires 6-year retention for tax-relevant records — this is well-established law",
      "UK civil limitation period is 6 years for contract claims — Limitation Act 1980",
      "GDPR does not mandate short retention where legitimate legal basis exists",
      "B2B customers contractually depend on delivery data for their own compliance",
      "EU customs regulations require multi-year record retention"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention periods for each specific EU member state the platform operates in",
      "Specific contractual retention obligations in existing B2B agreements",
      "Whether any data archival or export mechanisms exist that might partially mitigate the deletion",
      "The precise customs retention period per member state (ranges from 3 to 10 years)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Delivery records contain or are linked to VAT/tax-relevant transaction data",
      "The platform processes cross-border deliveries subject to customs regulations",
      "B2B service agreements include data availability commitments",
      "No separate archival system exists that would preserve records independently of this retention policy",
      "The 7-year retention period was originally chosen to satisfy the longest applicable statutory requirement"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes,' but auto-deleting delivery records after 90 days fundamentally changes the company's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations to B2B customers, and ability to defend claims. This is not an infrastructure decision — it is a legal and business policy decision that requires sign-off from Legal, Compliance, Finance, and affected B2B account managers. The framing must be corrected before any implementation proceeds.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal & Compliance", "Finance", "B2B Customers", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain the 7-year retention period",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — archive to cold storage after 90 days (reducing costs) but retain for the full legally required period per jurisdiction",
        "C: Engage Legal to define jurisdiction-specific minimum retention periods, then implement the shortest compliant period per record type"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — tiered storage. This achieves the legitimate goal of reducing hot-storage costs while maintaining full legal compliance. Cold/archival storage (e.g., S3 Glacier, Azure Cool) typically costs 80-90% less than hot storage, addressing the cost concern without creating regulatory exposure. Option C should follow as a refinement."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No legal review has been cited. The specific EU member states where the platform operates are not listed, making it impossible to enumerate exact per-jurisdiction retention requirements. The existing B2B contractual obligations around data availability are unknown.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal & Compliance", "B2B Customers"],
      "options": [
        "Obtain formal legal opinion on minimum retention periods per jurisdiction before proceeding",
        "Audit all B2B contracts for data retention and availability SLAs"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Both are prerequisites. No implementation should begin until Legal provides jurisdiction-specific retention requirements and B2B contracts are reviewed."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-deleting delivery records after 90 days violates statutory tax record retention requirements (HMRC: 6 years, EU member states: 5-10 years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "Destroying delivery records within the civil limitation period (6 years UK, up to 10 years EU) eliminates the company's ability to defend against legal claims", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "B2B customers depend on multi-year access to delivery data for their own compliance reporting; unilateral 90-day deletion likely breaches service agreements", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "The proposal has no documented GDPR-compliant lawful basis for choosing 90 days; GDPR does not require this and in fact legal retention obligations override it", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "Cross-border delivery records linked to customs declarations must be retained per EU Union Customs Code and UK equivalents — 90 days is grossly insufficient", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "The characterization as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect and dangerous — this is a compliance-critical policy change being misclassified as infrastructure work, bypassing required legal and business review", "business_rule_broken": "All" }
  ]
}
